title: Eigenvalues, the power method & SVD in MATLAB
summary: Master the exact MATLAB commands for eigenvalues and singular values: `eig` for all eigenvalues/eigenvectors, the hand-written power method `mypower.m` for the dominant eigenvalue (iteration, normalization, Rayleigh quotient, stopping and convergence), and `svd` for singular values and their use in computing rank, conditioning and least-squares solutions with `rank` and `pinv`. Grounded entirely in the Session 3 solution code and the FESTA slides.
objectives: Compute all eigenvalues with d = eig(A) and eigenvectors with [X,D] = eig(A)
Read and run mypower.m line by line: normalize, transform, Rayleigh quotient, relative stopping test
Predict whether the power method converges from the ratio |lambda_2|/|lambda_1| and from complex/opposite dominant eigenvalues
Compute the singular values with svd(A) and the full factors with [U,S,V] = svd(A)
Use the SVD relations: rank = number of nonzero singular values, K2(A) = s1/sn, and the rank-k truncation
Compute rank with rank(A) / rank(A,tol) and the minimum-norm least-squares solution with pinv(A)*b
Distinguish the backslash solution from pinv for rank-deficient / overdetermined systems

blocks.text: Why eigenvalues, the power method and the SVD live together

This session covers three linked tools. **Eigenvalues** of a square matrix $A$ are the numbers $\lambda$ for which $Ax=\lambda x$ has a non-zero solution $x$ (the eigenvector). The **power method** is a hand-written iteration (`mypower.m`) that finds only the eigenvalue of largest modulus, the spectral radius $\rho(A)$. The **SVD** generalizes eigenvalues to any matrix (even rectangular) and is the course's master tool for **rank**, **conditioning** and **least squares**. You must be able to type `eig`, `svd`, `rank`, `pinv` from memory and run `mypower` by hand.

Given $A\in\mathbb{R}^{n,n}$, a number $\lambda$ is an **eigenvalue** if there is a non-null vector $x$ with $Ax=\lambda x$; $x$ is the corresponding **eigenvector**. Equivalently $\lambda$ is an eigenvalue iff $A-\lambda I$ is singular, i.e. $\det(A-\lambda I)=0$. An $n\times n$ matrix has exactly $n$ eigenvalues (real or complex, not necessarily distinct); complex ones come in conjugate pairs because $A$ is real.
eig: all eigenvalues and eigenvectors

With one output `eig` returns the eigenvalues as a column vector; with two outputs it returns the eigenvectors too. The relation `A*X = X*D` holds: column `X(:,i)` is an eigenvector for the eigenvalue `D(i,i)`.

blocks.caption: [X,D] = eig(A): X has eigenvectors in its columns, D is diagonal with the eigenvalues.

blocks.title: eig on a 3x3 matrix
blocks.text:
From `session3_solutions_first_part.m`, the eigenvalues of the test matrix are obtained in one line:

```matlab
A_1 = [1 2 0; 1 0 0; 0 1 0];
eigenvalues_A_1_eig = eig(A_1)   % column vector of the 3 eigenvalues
```

To also get the eigenvectors:

```matlab
[X,D] = eig(A_1);
% X(:,i) is an eigenvector for the eigenvalue D(i,i)
```

The **spectral radius** (largest eigenvalue in modulus) is `max(abs(eig(A_1)))`, and the spectral norm is `norm(A_1,2)` or just `norm(A_1)`.
`eig` is the reference answer in the exercises: after running `mypower` you always compare its result to `eig(A)` to *justify* whether the power method converged and to which value.
The power method: what mypower.m computes

The power method approximates the eigenvalue of **largest modulus** $\lambda_1$ of a diagonalizable matrix, assuming it is unique: $|\lambda_1| > |\lambda_2| \ge \dots \ge |\lambda_n|$. Starting from a vector $z$, repeated multiplication by $A$ aligns the iterate with the dominant eigenvector $x_1$; we normalize each step to avoid overflow/underflow, and we read off the eigenvalue with the **Rayleigh quotient**.

blocks.caption: Power iteration: normalize, transform, then the Rayleigh quotient gives the eigenvalue estimate.

blocks.text: For a non-null $x$, $r_A(x)=\dfrac{x^{H}Ax}{x^{H}x}$. If $x$ is an eigenvector then $r_A(x)=\lambda$ exactly. In the power method, since $w$ is normalized, $w^{T}w=1$, so the quotient simplifies to $\lambda \approx w^{T}Aw = w^{T}(Aw)$ — exactly line 20 of `mypower.m`.

blocks.title: mypower.m, line by line

blocks.text: `lambda` is a **growing vector**, not a scalar: `lambda(1)=0` is the seed and `lambda(iter+1)` appends each new estimate. That is why the solution script reads the answer as `lambda_1(end)` and plots the whole convergence history with `plot(1:m1+1,lambda_1,'b+-')`. The stopping test compares `lambda(iter+1)` with the *previous* entry `lambda(iter)`.

blocks.title: Calling mypower (Exercise 4.1)

question.prompt: In `mypower.m`, what does the line `lambda(iter+1) = w' * w_star;` compute, given that `w_star = A*w` and `w` is already normalized?

options.content: The norm of the new vector
The Rayleigh quotient $w^{T}Aw$, i.e. the current eigenvalue estimate
The relative error of the iteration

question.explanation: Since `w_star = A*w`, the expression `w'*w_star` equals $w^{T}Aw$. Because `w` is normalized ($w^{T}w=1$), this is exactly the Rayleigh quotient, which estimates the dominant eigenvalue. It is not a norm (that is `norm`), not the error (computed two lines later), and not an eigenvector.

blocks.text: Convergence: read it off the eigenvalue ratio

The speed of the power method is governed by the ratio $|\lambda_2|/|\lambda_1|$ of the second-largest to the largest eigenvalue in modulus: the closer to 1, the slower. Two situations break convergence entirely: a dominant pair of **opposite** real eigenvalues ($\lambda_1=-\lambda_2$), and a dominant pair of **complex conjugates** — the real iteration cannot align with a complex eigenvector.

blocks.title: Three matrices, three behaviours (from the solution comments)
blocks.text:
```matlab
A_1 = [1 2 0; 1 0 0; 0 1 0];   % |l2|/|l1| = 1/2  -> converges in ~35 iters
A_2 = [0.1 3.8 0; 1 0 0; 0 1 0]; % |l2|/|l1| = 1.9/2 = 0.95 -> too slow, 100 iters not enough
A_3 = [0 -1 0; 1 0 0; 0 1 0];   % eigenvalues i and -i (modulus 1) -> does NOT converge
```

The course comments state exactly this: $A_1$ reaches `tol` in 35 iterations; $A_2$ converges so slowly that `m_max = 100` is insufficient; $A_3$ does not converge because it has the complex conjugate pair $i,-i$ of equal maximum modulus.

To justify a power-method result in the exam: compute `eig(A)`, look at the two largest moduli. If they are equal (complex pair, or opposite signs) -> no convergence. If close in size -> slow. The ratio $|\lambda_2|/|\lambda_1|$ is the convergence rate.

A related variant in the session is the **inverse power method** (`myinvers` in the script, `inverse_powers` in the slides). It finds the eigenvalue **closest to a shift $p$** by applying the power idea to $(A-pI)^{-1}$, returning $\lambda = p + 1/\mu$. It uses `eigs(A,1,p)` as the reference. It fails if $p$ is itself an eigenvalue (then $A-pI$ is singular) or if $p$ is equidistant from two eigenvalues.

`eigs` is the partial-eigenvalue command: `[X,D] = eigs(A,k)` gives the `k` largest-modulus eigenvalues; `eigs(A,k,p)` gives the `k` eigenvalues closest to `p`. The solution uses `eigs(A_1,3)` (all three) and `eigs(A_1,1,p)` (the one nearest the shift).
The SVD: singular values for any matrix

Every matrix $A\in\mathbb{R}^{m,n}$ factorizes as $A=USV^{T}$ with $U,V$ orthogonal and $S$ diagonal holding the **singular values** $s_1\ge s_2\ge\dots\ge s_p\ge 0$, $p=\min(m,n)$. The singular values are the square roots of the eigenvalues of $A^{T}A$. Crucially, computing singular values is **always well-conditioned**: $|s_i-\tilde s_i|\le\lVert A-\tilde A\rVert_2$.

blocks.tex: A = U S V^{T} = \sum_{i=1}^{p} s_i\, u_i v_i^{T}, \qquad s_1 \ge s_2 \ge \dots \ge s_p \ge 0
blocks.caption: SVD: U,V orthogonal, S diagonal of singular values in decreasing order.

blocks.title: svd commands
blocks.text:
```matlab
d = svd(A)            % column vector of singular values, decreasing order
[U,S,V] = svd(A)      % full factors: A = U*S*V'  (S same size as A)
```

`svd(A)` returns just the singular values; the three-output form returns the orthogonal `U`, the diagonal `S`, and the orthogonal `V` such that `A = U*S*V'`.

blocks.term: Rank, conditioning, low-rank truncation via SVD
blocks.text: If $s_1\ge\dots\ge s_r>s_{r+1}=\dots=0$ then: (1) the **rank** of $A$ is $r$ = number of non-zero singular values; (2) for a full-rank square $A$ the **2-norm condition number** is $K_2(A)=\lVert A\rVert_2\,\lVert A^{-1}\rVert_2 = s_1/s_n$; (3) the best rank-$k$ approximation in 2-norm is $A_k=\sum_{i=1}^k s_i u_i v_i^{T}$, with error $\lVert A-A_k\rVert_2 = s_{k+1}$.

blocks.title: Rank-k approximation and conditioning

question.prompt: Given the singular values $\sigma_1=10,\ \sigma_2=3,\ \sigma_3=1,\ \sigma_4=\tfrac12$ of $A\in\mathbb{R}^{4\times4}$, what is the 2-norm condition number $K_2(A)$?
question.explanation: For a full-rank matrix $K_2(A)=s_1/s_n$, the largest over the smallest singular value: $10/(1/2)=20$. Option B (10) is just $s_1$; A and C ignore that the smallest value $1/2$ doubles the ratio.

blocks.text: rank, pinv and least squares

`rank(A)` returns the number of singular values above a default tolerance; `rank(A,tol)` counts those above your `tol`. For an overdetermined / rank-deficient system $Ax=b$, `pinv(A)*b` gives the least-squares solution of **minimum Euclidean norm** via the SVD-based pseudo-inverse $A^{+}=VS^{+}U^{T}$.

`\` and `pinv` differ for non-full-rank systems. `x = A\b` returns a least-squares solution with the **maximum number of zero components** (a basic solution); `x = pinv(A)*b` returns the one with **minimum Euclidean norm**. They are equal only when $A$ has full column rank.

blocks.title: Stable least squares via truncated SVD (FESTA slide)

blocks.text: Summary of the command map you must memorize: `eig(A)` -> all eigenvalues; `[X,D]=eig(A)` -> eigenvectors+eigenvalues; `max(abs(eig(A)))` -> spectral radius; `eigs(A,k,p)` -> few eigenvalues near `p`; `mypower(A,z,tol,m_max)` -> dominant eigenvalue by iteration; `svd(A)` -> singular values; `[U,S,V]=svd(A)` -> full SVD; `rank(A,tol)` -> numerical rank; `cond(A)` or `s(1)/s(end)` -> conditioning; `pinv(A)*b` -> minimum-norm least squares.
